indicator_text: Replace the current system of hugely diverging bandwidths with a requirement that consultancies and law firms declare clients' expenditure in bandwidths of €10,000.
All registrants (including industry lobby groups, thinktanks and NGOs) should name all government agencies, grant-making foundations, companies and others that contribute to their budget.
Registrants should specify the amount received from each funding contributor.
Small private donations from individuals (up to a certain threshold) should be exempt from the funding-source disclosure requirement.
Registrants should be required to list the names of all their lobbyists in the register, not simply those with parliamentary access badges.
section: Names of lobbyists and revolving door listings

indicator_text: Registrants should disclose any former public offices that their lobbyists have held, including at the national level.
section: Names of lobbyists and revolving door listings

indicator_text: Organisations should be required to provide precise information on the main legislative proposals they are lobbying on, including a list of official legislative references.
Leaving the issues-lobbied field blank or providing a very general description should not be allowed.
Introduce a more user-friendly disclosure model for issues lobbied on, for example a drop-down list of legislative dossiers.
Consultancies and law firms should be obliged to provide a list of the main legislative proposals on which they have lobbied, for each of their clients.
The register secretariat should actively check entry updates and ensure financial information is up-to-date.
As a starting point, lobby expenses should refer to the most recent calendar year.
Require all registrations to be updated twice a year, on fixed and pre-determined dates.
Lobby consultancies and law firms should provide an up-to-date list of clients from the previous six-month period twice a year.
New clients should be declared within one month after the start of the contract.
Conduct regular checks on registrations and enforce against incomplete client lists and unrecognisable/ambiguous client-name abbreviations.
Consultancies and law firms that fail to disclose clients should be suspended from the register and have their Parliamentary passes withdrawn.

Reject any ad-hoc, derogative or exceptional formula for category I (law firms and lobby consultancies) entities claiming a need for client confidentiality; law firms providing lobbying services should be subject to the same transparency requirements as any other actor involved in lobbying.
Registrants should be required to report which lobby consultancies and law firms have assisted them in their lobbying.
section: Obliging registrants to disclose lobby consultancies and law firms assisting their lobbying

indicator_text: Registered organisations should disclose the total number of persons involved in lobbying, including their names.
section: Tackling the problem of under-reporting the number of lobbyists

indicator_text: Registrants should disclose a calculation of total full-time equivalents (FTEs) for the organisation, given that some employees may be engaged in interest representation only part of the time.
section: Tackling the problem of under-reporting the number of lobbyists

indicator_text: The register secretariat must enforce FTE calculation guidelines uniformly, not allowing different calculations to be used in combination with explanations from registrants.
section: Tackling the problem of under-reporting the number of lobbyists

indicator_text: Enable more comprehensive and effective data checking, including systematic checking of all new entries.
section: More comprehensive and effective data checking

indicator_text: Document changes in registrations with a flagging system that enables proper monitoring and allows the public to make comparisons both between organisations and over time.
The European Commission should provide comprehensive information online about all meetings and contacts between Commission officials and lobbyists.
Lobbying is a legitimate activity and an important part of the democratic process.
There is a significant public interest in ensuring the transparency and integrity of lobbying, as well as diversity of participation and contribution to public decision-making.
Any regulatory measures shall be proportionate, fit for purpose and not impede on the individual rights of assembly, free speech and petition of government.
'Lobbying' should cover any direct or indirect communication with a public official made, managed or directed with the purpose of influencing public decision-making.

'Public Official' shall include any individual with decision-making powers (and their advisors), elected/appointed/employed in executive or legislative branches at national, sub-national, or supra-national levels; in private bodies performing public functions; and in public international organisations domiciled or operational in the country.
'Lobbyist' should entail any natural or legal person who engages in lobbying activities, whether for private, public or collective ends, whether for compensation or without.

'Public decision-making' shall include creation/amendment of legislation or other regulatory measures; development, modification and implementation of public policies, strategies and programmes; and awarding of government contracts/grants, administrative decisions or other public spending decisions.

Citizen interactions concerning private affairs shall not be considered lobbying, save where they may concern individual economic interests of sufficient size or importance to potentially compromise public interest; in such case a balancing act on benefit and efficacy of regulation is needed, with consideration for constitutional protections.
If deemed necessary, the regulation may exclude public officials acting in their official capacity, diplomatic agents of foreign states, and political parties (since they play a quasi-public role).

Mandatory register: framework should require establishment of a mandatory lobbying register to which lobbyists, and all organisations who lobby (in-house or other), must sign up; activities are reported on a periodic basis along with designated supplementary information.

Timely registration and reporting: initial lobbyist registration should take place before any lobbying activity; updates shortly after relevant changes; activity reporting frequency set to allow meaningful analysis (minimum quarterly, ideally close to real-time).

Information disclosed: among others, the register shall include information on lobbyist identity, subject matter and outcomes sought, ultimate beneficiary, targeted institution/official, type and frequency of activities, supporting documentation, expenditure (incl. in-kind), funding sources, political contributions, prior public-official roles (incl. family), and public funding received.
Lobbyist identity
The subject matter of lobbying activities and outcomes sought
The ultimate beneficiary of lobbying activities (where relevant)
The targeted institution and/or the public official concerned
The type and frequency of lobbying activities
Any supporting documentation shared with the public officials
Lobbying expenditure, including in-kind (calculated to set criteria, in cost bands if need be)
Sources of funding, per client and per dossier
Any political contributions, including in-kind
Any prior roles as public official held by the individual and/or family members
Public funding received

Accessibility, openness and comparability of data: information online via single website, free of charge, indexable and downloadable in full as machine-readable open data (per opendefinition.org); should conform to existing open data standards; unique identifier per lobbyist/organisation; linking to other data sets is recommended.
Minimal administrative burden: system should allow ease of registration and reporting and minimise burden; balance with public-sector proactive disclosure obligations and the public right to know.
Access to information law: a comprehensive access-to-information law shall guarantee the public's right of access to information, including information about lobbying.

Limited exceptions: ATI law should set out a limited number of exceptions (privacy, security, financial sensitivity, decision-making process, other legitimate concerns), bound by a harm and public-interest test in line with international standards.

Proactive publication: public bodies/officials proactively publish organisational, programmatic, administrative, financial, and business-schedule information; meeting summaries; background documentation; preparatory analyses; obligations extend to expert/consultative bodies.

Decision-making footprint: where possible, and certainly for all legislative and policy initiatives, information should be clustered around individual items, producing a 'decision-making footprint' outlining history, public engagement and process; should link to lobbying-register data.

Clear, free and comprehensive: all information made public without charge (excluding actual delivery costs) and without limits to reuse; key information accessible/comprehensible to citizens and interest groups; recently generated data published as machine-readable open data.

Codes of Conduct (public officials): all public officials shall have an applicable set of rules laying out key standards of conduct including for dealings with third parties; rules may be institution-dependent but should include behavioural principles, record-keeping duty, duty to avoid unregistered lobbyists, confidentiality, conflict-of-interest mechanism, gifts/hospitality guidance, assets/interests disclosure.
Key behavioural principles including transparency, integrity, responsiveness, impartiality, fairness, accountability and serving the public interest
section: INTEGRITY > Public Officials > Codes of Conduct

indicator_text: The duty to keep a true and detailed record of their actions, including of meetings with lobbyists
section: INTEGRITY > Public Officials > Codes of Conduct

indicator_text: The duty to avoid lobbying contacts with unregistered lobbyists, and to report any violations of the lobbying rules to their superiors or relevant bodies
section: INTEGRITY > Public Officials > Codes of Conduct

indicator_text: The duty of confidentiality, subject to the public access to information regime
section: INTEGRITY > Public Officials > Codes of Conduct

indicator_text: A comprehensive mechanism for dealing with any real, potential or apparent conflicts of interest, including the incompatibilities of being a lobbyist
section: INTEGRITY > Public Officials > Codes of Conduct

indicator_text: A comprehensive guidance for dealing with gifts and hospitality, including their registration or decline
section: INTEGRITY > Public Officials > Codes of Conduct

indicator_text: A system for assets and interests disclosure by the public official, as well as by their family and business partners depending on the scope of decision-making power
section: INTEGRITY > Public Officials > Codes of Conduct

indicator_text: Post-employment restrictions: proportionate moratoria (or 'cooling-off periods') of at least 2 years before former public officials can lobby their former institutions concerning prior duties; approval from a designated ethics agency may be required.

Pre-employment restrictions: prospective public officials (excluding those elected), particularly those hired/seconded into advisory and regulatory roles, subject to a conflict-of-interest vetting process that may necessitate recusal, supervision, or disqualification; omission to declare relevant details should trigger disciplinary procedures.
Statutory code of conduct: there should be a statutory code of conduct for lobbyists developed in close consultation with all stakeholders and interested parties.
Behavioural standards: the code shall lay out core behavioural standards, particularly with regard to honesty and the avoidance of undue influence.

Self-regulation: supplementary measures shall be taken to encourage lobbyists to voluntarily adopt, publicise and report on additional ethical commitments, including through collective action; commitments backed by internal control/sanction mechanisms and integrated within CSR/governance strategy.

Right to participate: a generally recognised right for all groups and the public at large to participate in public decision-making, extending in particular to legislative and policy matters, within all levels of governance.
section: PARTICIPATION & ACCESS > Public Participation in Decision-making

indicator_text: Public consultation process: a legal framework should lay out, in a law or group of laws, the varied means for public participation in formulation, implementation, and evaluation of policies and laws — including timeframes, mechanisms to disseminate meeting information, attendance/participation rules, and tools to submit comments.
section: PARTICIPATION & ACCESS > Public Participation in Decision-making

indicator_text: Equal opportunity: there shall be an obligation on public authorities to provide an equal opportunity for participation to various interest groups and the public at large.
section: PARTICIPATION & ACCESS > Public Participation in Decision-making

indicator_text: Timely and effective contribution: public authorities should provide an adequate consultation period allowing sufficient time for review of preparatory materials, and should promote effective participation at the appropriate stage while decisions are still open.
section: PARTICIPATION & ACCESS > Public Participation in Decision-making

indicator_text: Publication of results: written and verbal views of consultation participants shall be made public; authorities should outline how the various views have been taken into account and why.
section: PARTICIPATION & ACCESS > Public Participation in Decision-making

indicator_text: Refusals of the right: public authorities shall provide a written justification for any refusal of the right to participate; those excluded shall have options to challenge and contest the exclusion in a procedure expeditious enough to allow participation if the refusal is overturned.
section: PARTICIPATION & ACCESS > Public Participation in Decision-making

indicator_text: Balanced composition: there should be a legal obligation on public authorities to strive for a balanced composition of expert and advisory bodies, representing a diversity of interests and views.
Disclosure of interests: advisory and expert group members shall be required to disclose their interests and affiliations relevant to items under consideration in advance of any work on the issue.
Lobbyist incentives: any lobbyist incentives should be considered with care, so as not to entrench special privilege of organised interests over those of individual citizens.

Management & Investigation: an independent, mandated and well-resourced oversight body or coordinated mechanism should be charged with managing registration, reviewing conflicts of interest, collating proactively disclosed information, monitoring compliance (incl. spot-audits), following up on complaints, investigating breaches, and consulting on/defining further provisions.
Managing lobbying registration

section: OVERSIGHT, MANAGEMENT AND SANCTIONS > Management & Investigation

indicator_text: Collating and disseminating the locations of proactively disclosed information

indicator_text: Monitoring compliance (including pro-active verification and spot-audits of reports)

indicator_text: Advice, analysis and awareness raising: the oversight body should also offer guidance and training to lobbyists and public officials, analyse trends and report findings, raise awareness among the public and the profession, and promote best practice.

Complaint mechanism: a well-publicised complaint mechanism that allows anyone to report violations openly, confidentially or anonymously, and to be informed on the specific outcome of the complaint, subject to privacy limitations.

Sanctions: lobbyists and public officials shall be subject to effective, proportionate and dissuasive sanctions for violations; sliding scale incl. criminal sanctions, (temporary) de-registration, and disciplinary proceedings; sanctioned names may be published; contracts concluded in violation may be deemed null and void; personal liability of leadership where they consented or connived.

Local context: insofar as not contrary to the guiding principles, lobbying regulations should address the particularities of the local context — socio-political considerations (corporatism, formalised social partnerships); degree of informal and professional lobbying; nature of public concerns potentially giving rise to regulation.

Broader regulatory framework: design of lobbying regulations necessitates thorough review and consistency with — and potential amendment of — the broader regulatory framework, in particular laws/policies on trading-in-influence, political finance, public procurement, media law, labour law, whistle-blower protection, legislative procedure, judicial/administrative review, and rights to free speech/assembly/petition.
Trading in influence, bribery and other corrupt conduct

Responsible lobbying must consider the wider public interest, not only an organisation's needs narrowly defined; it should respect the interests and needs of people, communities and the environment, and lobbyists should be able to present a public interest case for their positions.
Responsible lobbying should support an evidence-based approach to policy making, clearly respecting independent, peer-reviewed science.

Responsible lobbying must respect and not undermine existing public policy / human rights frameworks developed by international agencies, national and/or sub-national governments, and standard-setting bodies.
Political donations should at best be avoided; if made, they should be fully and transparently disclosed.
Conflicts of interest should be identified and disclosed with clear policies in place — including credible dispute resolution — to manage them.

The lobbying organisation should have clear codes of conduct to prevent bribery and corruption in its relations with public officials, including the offering and receiving of gifts, hospitality or other financial and in-kind incentives; codes apply to all in-house and intermediary lobbyists.

Policies and processes for managing the "revolving door" should be in place, covering the hiring of former politicians and public officials, secondments or placement of staff into public sectors, and cooling-off periods where needed.

Disclose the nature of all direct and intermediary lobbying (paid or unpaid), including: identity of lobbyists engaged; identity of public sector officials engaged; issues addressed; outcomes sought; positions on sub-national, national and international policy frameworks; frequency of engagement; and ultimate beneficiaries.

Disclose the monetary value of: spend on direct lobbying (including spend on intermediary lobbyists, paid or unpaid); spend on third-party organisation memberships (including the lobbying portion); and political donations (including in-kind), consistent with national frameworks.
Disclose any materials shared with public sector recipients as part of lobbying activity, excluding commercially sensitive materials.
Disclose the full list of third-party organisation memberships, including board seats held.
Disclose policies, processes and approaches, and results of accountability mechanisms such as periodic audits, referenced under other Principles of this Framework.
All disclosures should be made at least annually following reporting cycles, ideally quarterly.

All disclosures should be publicly available in an easily accessible and identifiable location on the organisation's website, and where relevant promptly made available to public sector officials lobbied.
All disclosures should be compiled in a dedicated report, report section or webpage, providing a total view of the organisation's material activities and issues, nationally and globally.

Responsible lobbyists will have controls over third-party membership organisations (such as trade associations): processes to identify positions across all geographies (taking into account credible media reports), and processes to review and proactively manage relationships, with clear escalation steps in cases of misalignment up to withdrawal.

Have controls over all lobbyists (in-house and intermediary, paid or unpaid) to ensure they understand and adhere to organisational policies — through codes of conduct, training, and regular performance assessment — with substantial public sanctions for those who contravene policies.
There will be external stakeholder involvement in lobbying — for example stakeholder partners may be involved in setting lobbying positions, agreeing lobbying strategy, and reviewing performance.

Support for and adherence to national laws, standards and any national codes of conduct for lobbyists, with any illegal and unethical activity duly reported to relevant legal or oversight authorities.

The organisation's Board to have clear oversight of lobbying policy positions, the lobbying processes and practices of the organisation itself, of intermediary lobbyists (paid or unpaid), and of the lobbying activity of third-party organisations it is a member of.

Periodic audits, the results of which should inform policies and be publicly reported, of: all public disclosures (for accuracy and completeness); all lobbying activities (for alignment); and controls over third-party membership organisations or intermediary lobbyists (for effectiveness).

Internal whistleblowing mechanisms, offering all employees the chance to raise anonymously any concerns or breaches of the organisation's lobbying policies and codes of conduct, or illegal/unethical lobbying activity.

Respect and safeguard the opportunity for interest groups to lobby — for example, not spending disproportionate resources on lobbying to crowd out other interests, regardless of legal caps on political donations.
Pursue opportunities for multi-stakeholder partnerships, including companies and CSOs, to lobby together where a common position that supports key public interest outcomes can be found.
Cooperation between companies and CSOs to promote and support higher transparency and accountability standards from governments.
lobbying means promoting specific interests by communication with a public official as part of a structured and organised action aimed at influencing public decision making.
lobbyist means any natural or legal person who engages in lobbying.
public decision making means decision making within the legislative and executive branches, whether at national, regional or local level.
public official means any person exercising a public function, whether elected, employed or otherwise, in the legislative or executive branches.
legal regulation means statutory regulation, a system of self-regulation or a combination of both.
Legal regulation of lobbying should promote the transparency of lobbying activities.
Consultant lobbyists acting on behalf of a third party should be subject to legal regulation.
section: B. Activities subject to legal regulation

indicator_text: In-house lobbyists acting on behalf of their employer should be subject to legal regulation.
section: B. Activities subject to legal regulation

indicator_text: Organisations or bodies representing professional or other sectoral interests should be subject to legal regulation.
section: B. Activities subject to legal regulation

indicator_text: Exemptions to legal regulations on lobbying should be clearly defined and justified.
section: B. Activities subject to legal regulation

indicator_text: Legal regulation of lobbying should not infringe the right of individuals to express their opinions and petition public officials, bodies and institutions, whether individually or collectively.
section: C. Freedom of expression, political activities and participation in public life

indicator_text: Legal regulation of lobbying should not infringe the right of individuals to campaign for political change and change in legislation, policy or practice within the framework of legitimate political activities, individually or collectively.
section: C. Freedom of expression, political activities and participation in public life

indicator_text: Information on lobbying activities in the context of public decision-making processes should be disclosed.
The rules on disclosure should be proportionate to the importance of the subject matter of the public decision-making process and should reflect constitutional guarantees.
A register of lobbyists should be maintained by public authorities or other designated bodies.
Information held in the register should be of a declaratory character. Lobbyists should be responsible for ensuring the information is accurate and up to date.
The register should be easily accessible and user-friendly. It should be available online with easy-to-use search facilities, open to the public, and consultation should be free of charge.
The processing of personal data from the register should comply with applicable standards on personal data protection.
The register should include as a minimum the name and contact details of the lobbyist.
The register should include as a minimum the subject matter of the lobbying activities.
The register should include as a minimum the identity of the client or employer, where applicable.
In order to further promote transparency, registers may include additional information in accordance with national conditions and requirements.

Where a member State can demonstrate that alternative mechanisms guarantee public access to information on lobbying activities and ensure equivalent levels of accessibility and transparency, it may be considered that the requirement for a public register is satisfied.
Lobbyists should be guided by the principles of openness, transparency, honesty and integrity.
section: F. Standards of ethical behaviour for lobbyists

indicator_text: Lobbyists should be expected to provide accurate and correct information on their lobbying assignment to the public official concerned.
section: F. Standards of ethical behaviour for lobbyists

indicator_text: Lobbyists should be expected to act honestly and in good faith in relation to the lobbying assignment and in all contact with public officials.
section: F. Standards of ethical behaviour for lobbyists

indicator_text: Lobbyists should be expected to refrain from undue and improper influence over public officials and the public decision-making process.
section: F. Standards of ethical behaviour for lobbyists

indicator_text: Lobbyists should be expected to avoid conflicts of interest.
section: F. Standards of ethical behaviour for lobbyists

indicator_text: Legal regulations on lobbying should contain sanctions for non-compliance. These sanctions should be effective, proportionate and dissuasive.
Appropriate measures tailored to national circumstances should be in place in order to avoid risks to public sector integrity that might be created by lobbying activities.

Measures could include a "cooling-off" period before either a public official may become a lobbyist after leaving public employment or office, or a lobbyist may become a public official after ceasing lobbying activities.
Measures could include guidance to public officials concerning refusing or disclosing the receipt of gifts and hospitality offered by a lobbyist.
Measures could include guidance to public officials concerning how to respond to communications from lobbyists.
Measures could include guidance to public officials concerning reporting violations of the regulations or rules of conduct on lobbying activities.
Measures could include guidance to public officials concerning disclosing conflicts of interest.
Measures could include guidance to public officials concerning preserving the confidentiality of data.
Oversight of the regulations on lobbying activities should be entrusted to designated public authorities.
Oversight may include monitoring compliance with the regulations.
Oversight may include providing guidance to lobbyists and public officials on the application of the regulations.
Oversight may include raising awareness among lobbyists, public officials and the public.
The framework for the legal regulation of lobbying activities should be kept under review.

The following types of lobbyists are included in the register: professional lobbyists/consultants, in-house company lobbyists, in-house organisation lobbyists, professional consultancies, law firms, think tanks, research institutions, public entities, government agencies/employees

Legislation increases the transparency and accountability of lobbying activities in a way that includes, but is not limited to, the issues set out in (b)–(i)

indicator_text: When an MP initiates a meeting with a minister or their adviser, the MP must disclose to the responsible minister's office whether the MP has a private interest in the matter (with 'private interest' defined to include donations or other benefits provided by a person or entity who asked the MP to make representations)

indicator_text: When an MP initiates a meeting with a minister or their adviser, the MP must disclose to the responsible minister's office the names of any persons or entities who have made representations to the MP requesting that they lobby the minister or their adviser (regardless of whether the MP has a private interest)

indicator_text: MPs initiating such meetings must maintain records of those disclosures in a form that is auditable and can be made available to appropriate entities, including the lobbying regulator

indicator_text: Lobbying regulation in the UK is among the very weakest of countries that have developed lobbying disclosure laws.
The set of lobby clients listed in the register is significantly different from the set of groups with whom ministerial offices report having met.
Collating information about ministerial meetings is too burdensome for any layperson to do.
The debate surrounding lobbying transparency as the law was being written made clear that the proposal was not robust enough.
Lobbying clients meet more frequently with Government ministers than unregistered groups, and a small number of lobby clients dominate over the others.

Accuracy (preciseness, factuality) of disclosed transparency data — does the disclosure offer policy-specific information rather than vague filler labels, and are organizational affiliations identified?
Accessibility of disclosed transparency data — is online access public and open, with no registration required, and are files downloadable?
Completeness of disclosed transparency data — are all required reports filed, and do the data cover all relevant levels of officials and all departments?
Understandability of disclosed transparency data — are reports written in non-technical language, avoiding uncommon acronyms?
Timeliness of disclosed transparency data — are quarterly reports uploaded promptly after the close of the relevant quarter?
Free or low cost of access to disclosed transparency data — apart from acquisition costs and internet access fees, is the information free?
Searchability of disclosed transparency data — does a complete, dedicated searchable database exist that supports comprehensive, comparative, and targeted queries?

Re-coding of CPI's "minimum threshold for money spent" item — UK lobby firms need not register if they do not have a VAT number (annual revenue ≥ £85,000); CPI gives 0 points if the threshold is higher than $500.
section: Re-application of CPI Hired Guns rubric to UK

indicator_text: Re-coding of CPI's "individual lobbyists must register" item — only consultant lobbying firms must register, not the individuals who work for them, nor in-house lobbyists; CPI gives 0 points if individual lobbyists are not required to register.
section: Re-application of CPI Hired Guns rubric to UK

indicator_text: The 5-year-old lobby register misses the majority of lobbying that occurs in London, especially lobbying by those firms and campaigning organizations that lobby on their own behalf; names, targets, and expenditures of lobbyists are not reported, no subject matter being discussed, and no limitations on gift-giving or revolving-door employment.

The Government's published data about ministers' meetings with outside groups, touted as transparency, are almost totally unusable as provided; applying Piotrowski & Liao (2012) plus Holman & Luneburg (2012)'s searchability criterion, the usability of meetings data is low.

Very little overlap between lobby register and ministerial meetings data — lobby register contains <4% of organizations known to have met with government ministries; meetings data contain no records for as many as 70% of clients known to have contracted with professional lobbying firms.
Tremendous skew in the distribution of ministers' meetings — majority of meetings held with just 10% of groups; more than a quarter of meetings with just 1% of groups.

Transparency: are the Ministry and banks transparent enough to protect citizens' right to know?
Openness to citizen input: are there sufficient opportunities for citizens to provide input into the financial legislative process to protect their right to be heard?
Equality of access: how is bank lobbying organised, does it result in privileged access and undue influence, and is there equality of access for citizens and civil society?
Balance and public interest: how are citizen interests and financial-sector interests weighed, and is the public interest protected?
Accountability: is there information and debate about ministerial decisions, and how accountable are the banks for their lobbying positions and activities?
Legally binding regulations should be introduced by national law to ensure equity among different stakeholders and citizens.
Creation of a mandatory Transparency Register with a code of conduct.
Implementation of a comprehensive legislative footprint (lobby paragraaf).
Establishing guidelines for all civil servants and ministers on dealing with lobbyists.
Transparency about Ministers' official diary.
Guidance for improving the balanced weighing of different interests with special attention to the public interest and the interests of citizens.
Mandatory comprehensive legislative footprint included in the Memorie van Toelichting accompanying the official internet consultation, clarifying external inputs received before consultation.
Footprint is comprehensive when sent to Parliament with policy papers / final legislative proposals, covering all formal and informal inputs (not only consultation responses).
Footprint explains how the Ministry has taken into account all formal and informal interactions and how the range of interests were balanced, including underrepresented interests.
Footprint based on renewed legislative drafting guidelines (aanwijzingen) ensuring fullest possible transparency without compromising whistle-blower access.

Public announcements about legislation as it is being developed (e.g. dedicated webpage with summary, monthly-updated list of external inputs received, links to consultation submissions) — a 'living legislative footprint'.
Decision-makers (Ministers, Deputy Ministers, Director-Generals, senior officials) publish diaries that announce as many meetings/events as possible a week ahead.

Maintain a detailed website archive of professional meetings/events with external parties, recording date, organisation/individuals attending, subjects covered, documents received before/during/after, location, links to speeches, and use of social media.
Improve public internet consultations by including questions accessible and comprehensible to citizens and non-profit organisations.

indicator_text: Identification of diverse stakeholders affected by each legislative proposal and improved impact-assessment of proposals on citizens and non-profit interests, developed in the Memorie van Toelichting guidelines.
Clarify the benefits and risks of legislation to the public interest.
Sufficient human resources to process diverse inputs and consultation responses, so responses do not delay or undermine legislation.
Clear and practical guidelines on balancing inputs from different stakeholders, e.g. to avoid the interest group with the most consultation responses dominating.
Memorie van Toelichting sent to Parliament explains how all formal and informal inputs were assessed and how underrepresented interests were considered.

Civil servants and policy makers ask industry-association representatives which members support a presented position, lobby paper, or consultation response, to avoid capture by a small subset of members.

Improved Memorie van Toelichting and comprehensive legislative footprint, mandatory transparency register, openness to citizens, up-to-date BNC-fiche, and improved weighing of interests should support parliamentary, media and civil-society debate about ministerial choices and clarify how the public interest was taken into account, who is impacted, and whether weighing favoured a particular stakeholder.

Publish a comprehensive Minister of Finance diary including meetings with external parties (inside and outside the Ministry), topics discussed (e.g. specific legislative proposal), and attendees at agreed meetings (excluding events / large meetings).
Publish on a Ministry-of-Finance webpage a list of all current EU legislative processes in which the Minister co-decides, with links to BNC-fiche, EC consultation, EP, and ECOFIN sites.
BNC-fiche starts with a short, easy-to-understand summary for non-experts.
BNC-fiche regularly updated to reflect changes in the Minister's position or compromises reached at ECOFIN.
Public official internet consultations are organised in due time about EU financial legislative proposals and the Minister's position on them.
Public consultations announced on the Ministry's home page.
Use a range of means to attract responses from academics, stakeholders and citizens.
Memorie van Toelichting includes details of external contacts made by the Ministry prior to website consultation.

Increase in-house capacity / human resources to identify and attract diverse non-financial interests into the decision-making process, implementing the new external-contacts guidelines (opletpunt, brede blik).

Analyse the balance of interests and powers (krachtenveldanalyse) represented in consultation responses, expert and consultation meetings, ministerial meetings, and other inputs; pro-actively search for missing stakeholder views and public-interest positions when imbalance is detected.

indicator_text: Bank gives details of all legislative proposals on which it has been active, either directly or indirectly via industry associations.
Bank gives details of lobbying activities on its website under easy-to-understand headings, with an easy-to-read short overview (position, legislators approached, main activities).
Bank defines what it means by the term 'lobbying'.
Publish all lobby papers and consultation responses; make them easy to find on the website.
List significant issues covered in consultation submissions with a link to a short summary, plus all related submissions and position papers, preferably with details of relevant lobbying activities.
Publish all memberships of financial industry associations in the annual report (even inactive memberships); refer in the GRI G4-16 index to the exact webpage where memberships are listed.
Submit a complete list of memberships to the EU Transparency Register and any future Dutch transparency register.
Ensure overview of memberships is available to all bank staff and provide management board with information on each industry association's lobbying position.
Publish information about the bank's level of engagement with each association (board member, working groups, committees, etc.).
Personnel budget and operating costs for the unit/department that lobbies and/or develops the bank's position, including external lobbyists.
Costs of membership of all financial industry organisations that represent or lobby on behalf of the bank (regardless of active involvement).
Cost of membership of lobby industry associations (public-affairs associations) to which in-house lobbyists belong as members.
Estimated costs of activities by bank representatives (from experts to management-board members) in relation to contact with legislative authorities.

Develop a code of conduct for those involved in lobbying, expertise sharing and representation, potentially incorporated into the Dutch bankers' oath; otherwise ensure all are subject to (preferably mandatory or statutory) codes of conduct.
Lobbying treated as a corporate-responsibility issue: all lobbying must be in line with the bank's sustainability statements/goals, with explanation when not.
Industry associations to which banks belong have strong codes of conduct for those who lobby on their behalf.
Disclose the names of the lobbyists who represent the bank at public institutions.
Comprehensive lobbying policy with guidelines covering transparency (positions, activities, costs, memberships).
Comprehensive lobbying policy covers integrity (which codes of conduct are followed).
Comprehensive lobbying policy covers CSR (no contradiction with sustainability objectives, no harm to society and public interest).
Comprehensive lobbying policy covers coordination and supervision of all lobbying positions.
Comprehensive lobbying policy defines who is finally responsible and accountable for lobbying positions and activities, and (if appropriate) for redressing excessive or undue behaviour.

Comprehensive lobbying policy covers level of engagement in, and responsibility for, positions and lobbying activities of financial industry associations, to avoid abuses of representation and excessive lobbying.

Data must be accessible: free to use and reuse, published in a timely manner and easily found.
Data must be accurate: complete and reflective of reality.
Data must be intelligible: structured in a way that can be analysed (e.g. clear and consistent columns, values and formats).
Data must be meaningful: useful for the user.
MEP declarations must include occupation or membership of the parliamentarian.
MEP declarations must include other mandates held by the parliamentarian.
MEP declarations must include membership or activity of the parliamentarian.
MEP declarations must include occasional activity if total remuneration exceeds €5,000 in a calendar year.
MEP declarations must include holdings or partnerships with potential public policy implications.
MEP declarations must include other financial interests of the parliamentarian.

Transparency Register entry must include registrant's key characteristics (e.g. address of headquarter and Brussels office, names of people with legal responsibility, name of person in charge for EU affairs).
Transparency Register entry must include goals/remit of lobbying activities.
Transparency Register entry must include specific activities and EU initiatives covered by the register.
Transparency Register entry must include number of persons and full-time equivalents involved in the lobbying activities.
Transparency Register entry must include names of persons accredited for access to EP premises.
Transparency Register entry must include membership and affiliation of the registrant.
Transparency Register entry must include estimated lobby expenses of the registrant.
Transparency Register entry must include grants received from EU institutions by the registrant.
Transparency Register entry must include financial data on the registrant.
Lobby meeting record must include the date of the meeting.
Lobby meeting record must include the location of the meeting.
Lobby meeting record must include the entity or entities met.
Lobby meeting record must include the subject(s) of the meeting.

indicator_text: The European Union should publish more detailed and standardised information on MEP declarations in machine-readable formats and through a centralised, searchable site.
The European Union should eliminate restrictions on the use and reuse of data.
The European Union should revise the financial thresholds in the declarations to allow for more accurate reporting.
The European Union should translate all declarations into all official EU languages.
The European Union should cross-check and validate declarations to make sure that they are plausible and accurate.
The European Union should move toward "live exporting" and real-time, automated updating of information from the Transparency Register, including the use of an API.
